metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'coaching remains non-prescriptive' with a concrete, deterministic check (e.g., 'the new MOVE_REGISTRY entry contains no imperative verb targeting a specific file or function name; verified by grep/review of the move text against the non-prescriptive template in MOVE_REGISTRY') or explicitly note it is verified by prompt-text code review.
findings.finding: AC5's 'coaching remains non-prescriptive' is a subjective quality term with no concrete proving command — there is no deterministic test or structured artifact check that could produce binary evidence of non-prescriptiveness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'coaching remains non-prescriptive (states the evidence expectation, not the implementation)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'verified by inspection: the new coaching move in MOVE_REGISTRY states the attestation evidence expectation (what evidence is needed) without naming a specific implementation file, function, or command the plan author must produce — confirmed by a reviewer checking the move text against the existing non-prescriptive moves in the registry'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the actual non-compliant DET emitter(s): the plan's 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' does not exist separately from P5 (already subject-emitting); re-ground COMPONENT 2 against a concrete emitter name found by the mandated audit, or explicitly state no non-compliant emitter exists and the backstop rule alone is the deliverable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' exists as a distinct DET emitter that omits subjects and needs fixing. A grep/read of det_floor.py finds no such detector: the only sibling/file-overlap emitter is the P5 interference branch inside `p5_task_dag` (det_floor.py:491-509), which the plan simultaneously declares already-compliant ('ALREADY emits subjects — it is NOT a target'). The motivating example from the Why section ('Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge — naming no tickets/files, unadjudicable') is the exact text of the P5 interference finding (line 499), meaning the plan's target emitter and its carve-out are the SAME detector. There is no separate 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' to fix.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET finding content floor; plan text 'The fix targets the emitters that genuinely omit subjects despite computing them (the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags)'
findings.suggested_fix: Run the mandated DET-emitter audit first and name the concrete emitter(s) found non-compliant (by detector name + det_floor.py line range). If the audit finds none, scope COMPONENT 2 down to the backstop hygiene rule only and remove the 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' fix claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the nonexistent 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' naming in COMPONENT 2 with the actual emitter id(s) the audit is expected to flag, or clarify that the audit may return zero non-compliant emitters (and the backstop alone is sufficient).
findings.finding: The plan names 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector' as the primary fix target for COMPONENT 2's subject-emission audit, but no such detector exists in the codebase. P6 in det_floor.py is the AC/DD quality checker (compound criteria, vague lexicon, verify-command lint); it has no sibling-overlap or file-interference logic. The sole sibling/file-overlap DET emitter is the P5 interference check (det_floor.py:491-509, `_file_interference`), which the plan explicitly excludes as 'NOT a target.' The audit directed at 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags' will find no such entity, leaving Component 2 without a concrete non-P5 fix target.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET finding content floor, plan text: 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags'
findings.suggested_fix: Since the P5 interference emitter already emits subjects, and P6 has no sibling-overlap logic, revise the plan to say: 'The audit targets all DET emitters other than P5-interference; if the audit finds no non-compliant emitters, only the hygiene backstop is implemented.' Remove the 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' reference entirely, as it names no existing entity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the routing for `absence_confirmed_in_context == 'insufficient'` in the Pass-3 veto rule — either 'abstain from veto (finding survives, same as a missing answer)' or 'treat as not-confirmed (veto fires same as no)' — and add this case to the regression fixture.
findings.finding: The `absence_confirmed_in_context` binary has a three-value vocabulary (`yes/no/insufficient`) but the Pass-3 veto rule only names the `no` branch (`claims_absence == yes AND absence_confirmed_in_context == no → dropped`). The plan states 'Missing answers abstain (no effect)' but that covers absent keys, not a present `insufficient` answer — the routing for `insufficient` is genuinely undefined, leaving a third-of-three enum value without a specified decision outcome.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass-3 veto rule (decide.py:318-326 analogy) and the binary vocabulary definition ('yes/no/insufficient')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to COMPONENT 1: 'insufficient (cannot determine) → abstain from veto (finding survives, same as a missing key); only an explicit no confirms the absence and fires the drop.' This mirrors the abstain-safe design of the existing plan-v2 axes.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan the default scope assumption (e.g., 'the sibling-overlap detector is the only known non-compliant emitter; any additional peers found by audit are in-scope but must be listed in the change record before implementation begins'), so the executor has a decided baseline rather than an open-ended investigation.
findings.finding: COMPONENT 2's audit scope defers the determination of which DET emitters to fix to the executor at runtime — 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags' means the actual fix targets are unknown until investigation occurs, making this a placeholder scope bullet.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET finding content floor, paragraph 1: 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags' with 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector (the only currently-known non-compliant emitter); if the audit identifies additional non-compliant peers, list them in the change record before patching them, capped at det_floor.py DET emitters only'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `claims_absence` and `absence_confirmed_in_context` are non-GRADED extras (like `cited_reference_accurate`, added outside the `GRADED_BINARY` loop and absent from `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`) or GRADED fields — and add the rationale.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies whether `claims_absence` and `absence_confirmed_in_context` join `GRADED_BINARY` (participating in validity scoring) or are non-GRADED extras like `cited_reference_accurate`. This is a schema placement gap: the existing `_build_binary()` in `verify.py` loops over `GRADED_BINARY` for graded fields and hard-codes `cited_reference_accurate` outside that loop as a veto-only field. Placing the new fields incorrectly would either inflate/deflate validity (if accidentally added to `GRADED_BINARY`) or leave their abstain-safe defaults inert with respect to validity.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to COMPONENT 1: 'Both new binaries are NON-GRADED — added to `_build_binary()` alongside `cited_reference_accurate`, outside the `GRADED_BINARY` loop, so they never participate in validity() and their defaults never affect the validity score.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the fallback behavior for the `operator_attested` tag scan in `plan_review_decide` when the scan errors or finds an ambiguous/near-miss tag — e.g. 'fail-open: treat as codebase-verifiable (operator_attested=False), log a warning, never raise.'
findings.finding: The `operator_attested` DET detection is specified as 'pure DET (exact case-insensitive prefix per ADR 0043)' injected in `plan_review_decide`, but the plan never specifies what happens when the plan text contains malformed or near-miss tags (e.g. `[operator_attested]` with underscore, which ADR 0043 already classifies as codebase-verifiable). The injection point in `plan_review_decide` is a step that runs after DET; if the tag scan raises or returns an unexpected shape, there is no stated fail-open behavior for this new detection path — unlike the DET floor's established `abstain` pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that verifies the audit record exists in the change (e.g. a comment, doc block, or CHANGES entry naming the non-compliant emitters found and fixed).
findings.finding: The plan body (Component 2) explicitly commits to recording an audit artifact — 'record the audit result (which emitters were non-compliant) in the change' — but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that this artifact exists or contains the required content, leaving it as uncovered work.
findings.location: Component 2 (DET finding content floor) — 'record the audit result (which emitters were non-compliant) in the change'; Acceptance Criteria section (no covering AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '[ ] The change includes a recorded audit result (comment, doc block, or CHANGES entry) naming which DET emitters were found non-compliant and which were already compliant; the P5 interference detector is explicitly listed as already compliant (not a target).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured log emit (debug/info level) at each of the three new drop points — absence-claim veto in decide.py, DET hygiene backstop in det_floor.py, and ac_unverifiable key removal in workflow_ops.py — recording enough context (finding index, drop reason, the binary values or field state that triggered the drop) for post-hoc diagnosis.
findings.finding: The three new silent-drop code paths — (a) absence-claim veto firing in decide.py, (b) DET hygiene backstop dropping a subject-less finding in det_floor.py, and (c) ac_unverifiable key removal in workflow_ops.py — have no mention of logging, metrics, or any audit trail. When any of these paths fires in production, there is currently no way for an operator to observe that a finding was dropped by the new logic rather than never generated.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 Pass-3 veto (decide.py:318-326 analog); COMPONENT 2 DET backstop (det_floor.py det_blocking_findings/det_advisory_findings); COMPONENT 3 workflow_ops.py:324
findings.suggested_fix: At each drop site, emit a structured log line such as: logger.debug('finding_dropped', reason='absence_claim_veto', finding_index=i, claims_absence=v1, absence_confirmed=v2) / logger.debug('det_finding_dropped', reason='no_location_no_evidence', finding_id=...) / logger.debug('ac_unverifiable_cleared', reason='operator_attested', finding_index=i). Add a counter metric (e.g., findings_dropped_reason_total with a reason label) if the system already has a metrics registry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion for `absence_confirmed_in_context`: cite an eval result, a few-shot fixture, or an explicit failure-mode bound that grounds the claim that the verifier can reliably match paraphrastic plan provisions to an absence claim.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the Pass-2 verifier can reliably answer `absence_confirmed_in_context` — a semantic lookup ('is there any provision in the plan text matching the item claimed absent?') — but cites no eval evidence or prior experiment validating this capability. The original false-positive (f1fa A1 verdict) was caused by the verifier failing to match semantically equivalent plan text to the finding's claimed absence. The new binary asks the verifier to perform exactly that matching task again, only now in a separate sub-question. If the verifier cannot do it reliably, the veto fires on false negatives (real absences read as 'confirmed') or still misses false-positives when the text equivalence is paraphrastic rather than lexical.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — absence-claim grounding veto / Pass 2 binaries: `absence_confirmed_in_context`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the three behavior-changing components (absence-claim veto, DET backstop, ac_unverifiable clearing) — e.g., a per-component feature flag, an expand-contract deployment sequence, or a documented 'revert to commit X' procedure — so that incorrectly-dropped findings can be restored without a full re-design cycle.
findings.finding: The plan introduces three behavior changes — (1) absence-claim veto drops previously-blocking findings, (2) DET hygiene backstop drops previously-surviving DET findings, (3) ac_unverifiable clearing changes verdict outcomes for operator-attested ACs — without providing any explicit rollback/back-out plan or feature-flag/expand-contract sequencing. Byte-compat tests (AC1, AC6) verify non-regression for unchanged paths but are not a reversibility mechanism; if the new veto or backstop incorrectly drops legitimate findings in production, there is no documented path to revert or disable the behavior without a new deployment.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 (absence-claim veto, decide.py), COMPONENT 2 (DET backstop, det_floor.py), COMPONENT 3 (ac_unverifiable clearing, workflow_ops.py); Acceptance Criteria AC2/AC3/AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single boolean config flag (e.g., ENABLE_ABSENCE_CLAIM_VETO, ENABLE_DET_HYGIENE_BACKSTOP) defaulting to True that can be set to False in the deployment environment to immediately restore pre-change behavior; document the flag in Scope and add a test asserting that flag=False restores the prior code path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) amend ADR-0043 to cover the operator_attested injection pattern and ac_unverifiable clearing, and add a brief ADR or doc section for the absence-claim veto contract fields; or (b) explicitly state in Scope why no doc update is needed (e.g., 'these are internal implementation details fully described in this plan and the regression fixture').
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new verification contract shape (two new binary fields with defined semantics), a new absence-claim veto architecture, and a new operator_attested injection pattern in workflow_ops.py — none of which are accompanied by a stated ADR update, a doc pointer, or an explicit declaration that no doc update is needed. ADR-0043 (operator-attested tag) and ADR-0008 (independence invariant) are cited but not flagged for update, leaving the new contract fields and veto mechanism undocumented at the architectural level.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 (verify.py contract fields), COMPONENT 3 (workflow_ops.py injection pattern); Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a CLAUDE.md / design-doc section) entry capturing the three new patterns introduced by this plan: (1) the absence-claim grounding veto and its abstain-safe contract, (2) the DET-scoped hygiene backstop and its non-touch guarantee for LLM-tier, and (3) the operator_attested upstream key-removal contract; include this artifact in Scope.
findings.finding: Three novel architectural decisions introduced by the plan — the absence-claim grounding veto pattern (a new Pass-3 deterministic drop axis), the DET-tier hygiene backstop rule (drop DET findings with no location AND no evidence spans), and the operator_attested injection mechanism (upstream key-removal before impact_plan) — are not captured in any ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc. The Scope section lists no doc artifact, and 'Design approved by operator 2026-07-12' in the preamble is an approval note, not an architectural record.
findings.location: Scope section; COMPONENT 1 Pass-3 veto; COMPONENT 2 DET backstop; COMPONENT 3 operator_attested injection
findings.suggested_fix: Create a single new ADR (e.g., ADR-0044) that documents: the absence-claim veto axis and why it uses a binary drop rather than a validity adjustment; the DET-tier backstop and its scoping invariant; and the operator_attested injection pattern and the 'clear upstream, not inside impact_plan' contract. Reference ADR-0043 and ADR-0008 as predecessors. Add the ADR path to the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether the three components (absence-claim veto, DET content floor, operator-attested AC awareness) should be split into separate tickets, given each is independently valuable, testable, and targets a distinct subsystem — or explicitly document why batching them together is the correct tradeoff (e.g. shared test gate, operator-approved grouping, all sub-session-sized individually).
findings.finding: The plan bundles three independently-releasable outcomes with three distinct reasons-to-change: (1) the absence-claim veto fixing a false-absence bug in verify.py/decide.py, (2) the DET content floor fixing contentless emitters in det_floor.py, and (3) the operator-attested AC awareness adding a new detection/injection/suppression path in workflow_ops.py and rubric prompts. Each component touches a different subsystem, has its own AC lines, and could be shipped, reviewed, and tested in isolation without the others.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 / COMPONENT 2 / COMPONENT 3 sections; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: If the components remain bundled, add a note in the plan explicitly acknowledging the decomposition choice (e.g. 'These are batched because each alone is sub-session-sized and they share a single test/lint gate; they are not independently releasable in practice because the shared regression fixture covers all three'). If they are split, Component 1 (the confirmed production regression fix) should ship first as it has the highest urgency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the key-removal suppression idiom vs the alternative of a symmetric veto binary (like `cited_reference_accurate`) or a boolean guard injected alongside churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface — document why upstream key-removal is preferred over those established patterns.
findings.finding: COMPONENT 3 suppresses `ac_unverifiable` by removing (or setting to `None`) the key from `severity_attributes` in `plan_review_decide`, upstream of `impact_plan`. This key-removal idiom is architecturally novel: the only precedent for DET-enrichment in `workflow_ops` (churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface) INJECTS new keys; it never removes existing ones. The plan does not acknowledge this asymmetry or justify why key-removal was chosen over an equally available alternative — e.g. a symmetric veto binary (analogous to `cited_reference_accurate`) inside `pass3_decide`, or a boolean guard injected the same way churn90 is injected. Key-removal is a destructive mutation of the verification result dict that has no established prior-art pattern in this codebase.
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 — operator-attested AC awareness / MECHANISM section; plan_review_decide (workflow_ops.py:324)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
